category_summary:
**Agentic loops** dominated conceptual discussion. **Swyx** led with an [essay on "Loopcraft"](/?date=2026-06-13&category=social#item-7165e832030d), arguing the central skill of the coming era is stacking, ascending, and descending recursive loops—a theme echoed by **Jerry Liu**, **Matt Shumer**, and **Yohei Nakajima**. Swyx also detailed his motivation for [building a vibecoding platform](/?date=2026-06-13&category=social#item-e24c68b74dea) that closes the error-fix loop.

- **AI coding capability** drew strong engagement: **Ethan Mollick** showed Claude Code with Fable [rebuilding the lost SimRefinery game](/?date=2026-06-13&category=social#item-e54c46debd55) into a playable sim. A CMU benchmark, **SusVibes**, [exposed widespread security flaws](/?date=2026-06-13&category=social#item-3b2f3460eed7) in functionally-passing coding-agent output. **OpenAI** rolled out [bankable Codex rate-limit resets](/?date=2026-06-13&category=social#item-dbb3b405a57a).
- **Eval integrity** sparked debate as **Clément Delangue** argued benchmarks [structurally favor closed-source APIs](/?date=2026-06-13&category=social#item-bc25dfa2955d) that route, fallback, and ensemble opaquely.
- **New model releases** featured **MiniMax M3** [(1M-context multimodal MoE)](/?date=2026-06-13&category=social#item-8d7a75f4d390) and **[Kimi K2.7-Code](/?date=2026-06-13&category=social#item-b4be8abc59b3)**, with **NVIDIA** unveiling [AgentPerf](/?date=2026-06-13&category=social#item-c024409d32c4), the first agentic AI infrastructure benchmark.

- On research, **Mollick** highlighted a striking finding that frontier general LLMs [outperform dedicated clinical tools](/?date=2026-06-13&category=social#item-76e57b04b7ab) like OpenEvidence. **Gary Marcus** stoked the AI-economics bubble narrative, flagging reports of **Meta** [cutting its Anthropic token budget](/?date=2026-06-13&category=social#item-7542b9886195).

items.title:
## On Loopcraft

One might argue the entire game of the next century is to be able to stack loops as...
items.content:
## On Loopcraft

One might argue the entire game of the next century is to be able to stack loops as effectively as possible. 

In the early days of each phase, it will be valuable to know when to go **DOWN** a loop when things go wrong (for reliability)… 

but it will probably be more valuable to know how to go **UP** a loop as models improve (for leverage).

If you don’t figure out how to do this, don’t be salty when you lose to those that do.

items.title:
A new benchmark just exposed the dirty secret behind every coding agent. 

Millions of developers no...
items.content:
A new benchmark just exposed the dirty secret behind every coding agent. 

Millions of developers now let AI agents write entire features unsupervised.
 
A Carnegie Mellon paper tested whether that code is safe to ship.

The team built SusVibes, a benchmark of 200 real coding tasks.
 
Each task came from open-source projects where humans once shipped vulnerabilities.

Agents had to edit around 170 lines across multiple files.
 
SWE-Agent running Claude 4 Sonnet passed functional tests 61% of the time.

Only 10.5% of its solutions were actually secure.
 
Over 80% of the working code contained vulnerabilities.

The researchers tried three obvious fixes:
 
1. Adding security warnings to prompts 
2. Asking agents to spot weaknesses
3. Revealing the exact vulnerability type
 
Security barely improved.

Functional accuracy dropped seven points.
 
The agents optimize for passing tests, not protecting your systems.

Would you ship agent code without a security review?
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: Summary of a Carnegie Mellon benchmark called SusVibes testing whether coding-agent output is secure, finding that while SWE-Agent on Claude 4 Sonnet passed 61% of functional tests, only about 10% of solutions were secure and over 80% of working code contained vulnerabilities; prompt-based fixes barely helped.
items.reasoning: Technically substantive coverage of a credible academic benchmark with concrete, surprising findings relevant to the widespread use of coding agents; moderate engagement.

items.title: the #1 thing that is driving me to build my own vibecoding platform rn is that none of them - and i ...
items.content:
the #1 thing that is driving me to build my own vibecoding platform rn is that none of them - and i lov vercel, cloudflare, netlify etc - none of them really close the loop for you in terms of setting you on the right path with errors and pinging you when shit fails (shit always fails)  

there's way too much "webmaster" infra to setup for every single project and i just want to do it once and for all, instead i'm being asked to npx posthog wizard here and npx arize skills there and it all just needs to be swallowed up into One Thing.

items.title:
The first agentic AI infrastructure benchmark is here.

An AI agent chains dozens to hundreds of AI ...
items.content:
The first agentic AI infrastructure benchmark is here.

An AI agent chains dozens to hundreds of AI model calls together, using tools, gathering context, and iterating until the task is done. Existing benchmarks weren't designed for that. 

AgentPerf from @ArtificialAnlys gives developers, enterprises and infrastructure providers a clear way to compare accelerated computing systems for agentic AI.

First round of results highlight that NVIDIA Blackwell delivers 20x more agents per megawatt than NVIDIA Hopper.

items.title: 🎉 Congrats to @MiniMax_AI on releasing MiniMax M3! Frontier coding and agentic capabilities, native ...
items.content:
🎉 Congrats to @MiniMax_AI on releasing MiniMax M3! Frontier coding and agentic capabilities, native image and video input, computer use, and a 1M-token context window, all in a single open model.

At the heart of M3 is MSA, a new sparse attention architecture: instead of attending densely over the full KV cache, each query scores 128-token KV blocks and runs attention only over the top blocks. That is what makes 1M-token context practical to serve.

M3 runs in vLLM with day-0 support, verified on NVIDIA and AMD hardware:
✨ MSA sparse attention with dedicated prefill and decode kernels
✨ 1M-token context serving with prefix caching and chunked prefill
✨ BF16 and MXFP8 checkpoints, with MoE backends for both Hopper and Blackwell
✨ Native multimodal input (image + video)
✨ Tool calling, reasoning parsing, and thinking-mode control for agent workloads

Day-0 support like this is a true team effort. Grateful to the teams at @MiniMax_AI, @NVIDIAAI, @AIatAMD, and @inferact, and to the vLLM community for making it happen. 🙏

Deep dive into the implementation, kernel work, and deployment recipes:

🔗 https://t.co/TbEc9VgqJ7
items.author: @vllm_project
items.summary: Following yesterday's [Reddit](/?date=2026-06-12&category=reddit#item-aeee70a21143) buzz about the planned MiniMax M3 release, vLLM details MiniMax M3's features: frontier coding/agentic capabilities, native image/video input, computer use, 1M-token context, and MSA sparse attention scoring 128-token KV blocks, with day-0 vLLM support across NVIDIA and AMD hardware.
items.reasoning: Technically rich breakdown of a new open model's architecture with good engagement, high relevance to inference community.

items.title:
It is really hard to combine research and product. 

A lot of the traditional wisdom in building pro...
items.content:
It is really hard to combine research and product. 

A lot of the traditional wisdom in building product at a startup don't apply for research. Building pure product requires scrappily building MVPs, iterating as quickly as possible on customer feedback, and being able to change direction in order to align within ICP needs. The product that satisfies customer needs doesn't *necessarily* need the latest scientific discovery. 

Research requires longer-term thinking. It requires dedicated focus time for deep exploration, synthesis, and experimentation in order to iterate on a deep piece of tech. It requires a longer-term bet and can't be disrupted on a whim by customer feedback. To some extent in order to do successful research, you have to basically ignore most/all customer feedback in order to focus on a core set of research goals. You care about general insights, not overfitting to a specific datapoint/bespoke need. In a vacuum where you only do research, the risk of course is that you create a beautiful piece of tech that doesn't have PMF. 

We've experienced this firsthand at @llama_index, where we *have* to do applied research to drive the frontiers of document understanding. But that means we have to simultaneously balance a wide volume of customer needs with a focused effort on improving the core pareto frontier of cost-accuracy.
items.author: @jerryjliu0
items.summary: Jerry Liu reflects on the tension between research and product, noting product needs rapid customer-driven iteration while research needs long-term focus and the freedom to ignore immediate feedback.
items.reasoning: Thoughtful original essay from a credible AI startup founder with solid engagement and broad relevance to AI orgs.

items.title: Many people are finding that the advantages of Fable often aren’t worth the cost. (See data below fo...
items.content:
Many people are finding that the advantages of Fable often aren’t worth the cost. (See data below for a sample analysis.)

This doesn’t mean that Fable doesn’t have its uses – clearly it does.

But it poses a *serious* problem for massive data center investments.

It suggests that newer, immense models will be niche that customers usually won’t pay for. Even bigger models might not be worth building at all, economically, because if they are even more expensive even fewer user will use them.

For a lot of purposes open models will suffice. (For some  purposes in which the cost of error is high, no models may suffice.)  In some cases people may not even need cloud, and prefer on-premie, especially as models become more optimized for local use.

If the above scenario is true, you have to ask whether we need quite so many data centers.

If we don’t, we end up with an oversupply.

The fallout across the economy is likely be immense.
items.author: @GaryMarcus

items.reasoning: Substantive multi-point economic argument tying model cost-efficiency to data center investment risk, with solid engagement.
items.summary_html: Marcus argues that the advantages of the Fable model often are not worth the cost, suggesting bigger models may stay niche, open models suffice for many tasks, and data center overbuilding could cause an oversupply with broad economic fallout.
items.content_html:
Many people are finding that the advantages of Fable often aren’t worth the cost. (See data below for a sample analysis.)

This doesn’t mean that Fable doesn’t have its uses – clearly it does.

But it poses a *serious* problem for massive data center investments.

It suggests that newer, immense models will be niche that customers usually won’t pay for. Even bigger models might not be worth building at all, economically, because if they are even more expensive even fewer user will use them.

For a lot of purposes open models will suffice. (For some purposes in which the cost of error is high, no models may suffice.) In some cases people may not even need cloud, and prefer on-premie, especially as models become more optimized for local use.

If the above scenario is true, you have to ask whether we need quite so many data centers.

If we don’t, we end up with an oversupply.

The fallout across the economy is likely be immense.

items.title:
We finally know why bigger models are smarter. It's not the data. 

More training data was supposed ...
items.content:
We finally know why bigger models are smarter. It's not the data. 

More training data was supposed to fix small models. 

A new paper shows why it cannot.
 
Researchers proved some tasks need model scaling, not data scaling. 

A small model fails them even with infinite data.
 
The cause is competition over neurons. 

Frequent tasks grab capacity first and keep it.
 
Rare task updates get overwritten before the next example arrives. 

The model learns, then forgets, in an endless loop.
 
Scaling breaks the loop in three steps:
 
1. Common tasks get fully learned
2. Their gradients fade to nothing
3. Rare features accumulate safely
 
The team pretrained OLMo models from 4M to 4B parameters. 

They injected novel tasks at controlled frequencies during training.
 
Only the largest models learned the rare ones. 

Interference between their gradients nearly disappeared.
 
How many tasks is your model silently skipping?

items.title: There's got to be some major advances in the data ingestion, indexing, and retrieval layer to make t...
items.content:
There's got to be some major advances in the data ingestion, indexing, and retrieval layer to make this happen.

Agents are getting much more sophisticated in fetching diverse sources of context with simple tool interfaces (e.g. CLI + filesystem). But: 
*  It is a known problem that agentic federated search through MCP isn't optimal for retrieval relevance 
* Agentic search is generally really slow without pre-indexing
* When you interleave diverse sources of data, you need to weight each item properly 
* Different types of data require different query interfaces - from SQL to embedding search

Looking forward to seeing how this materializes. Our main focus today is unlocking document-based context and we'd be keen to see how this integrates with SaaS/structured data.
items.author: @jerryjliu0
items.summary: Jerry Liu outlines needed advances in data ingestion, indexing and retrieval for agents, noting federated MCP search hurts relevance, agentic search is slow without indexing, and diverse data sources require weighting and different query interfaces.
items.reasoning: Substantive technical thread on agentic retrieval challenges from a credible RAG-focused founder with decent engagement.
items.summary_html: Jerry Liu outlines needed advances in data ingestion, indexing and retrieval for agents, noting federated MCP search hurts relevance, agentic search is slow without indexing, and diverse data sources require weighting and different query interfaces.

Agents are getting much more sophisticated in fetching diverse sources of context with simple tool interfaces (e.g. CLI + filesystem). But:

* It is a known problem that agentic federated search through MCP isn't optimal for retrieval relevance

* Agentic search is generally really slow without pre-indexing

* When you interleave diverse sources of data, you need to weight each item properly

* Different types of data require different query interfaces - from SQL to embedding search

Looking forward to seeing how this materializes. Our main focus today is unlocking document-based context and we'd be keen to see how this integrates with SaaS/structured data.

items.title: To people in the answers saying "but opus 4.8 is weaker so without fallback, the score would even be...
items.content:
To people in the answers saying "but opus 4.8 is weaker so without fallback, the score would even be higher": this is not necessarily true because of how any benchmark - which is an average of queries - work and what is called "the fallacy of division".

Even if Opus 4.8 has a lower average score on AA than Fable 5, it actually performs better than Fable 5 on some benchmark that compose the index of AA, especially where there's high refusal rate of Fable 5 (ex GPQA Diamond, AA-Omniscience). The same would go if you'd take a single benchmark btw as it's always an average of queries and the fact that a model has a higher score on average doesn't mean they answer better on 100% of queries.

So it's possible that Fable with Opus 4.8 fallbacks is getting a higher score than pure Fable, even if Opus 4.8 is weaker on average.

The challenge is no one knows, except the API provider, which is the challenge I'm pointing out.

More details below from Fable (or Opus?) themselves!
items.author: @ClementDelangue
items.summary: Delangue explains the fallacy of division in benchmarks, arguing a Fable setup with Opus 4.8 fallbacks could score higher than pure Fable even if Opus 4.8 is weaker on average, since indexes average over queries and refusal rates vary.
items.reasoning: Technically substantive methodological argument from a credible industry leader with good engagement; addresses real benchmark interpretation pitfalls.
items.summary_html: Delangue explains the fallacy of division in benchmarks, arguing a Fable setup with Opus 4.8 fallbacks could score higher than pure Fable even if Opus 4.8 is weaker on average, since indexes average over queries and refusal rates vary.

items.title: Fable is clearly the best model ever released, but imo the pushback around it is a product packaging...
items.content:
Fable is clearly the best model ever released, but imo the pushback around it is a product packaging issue.

No one wants to feel like they have to tune thinking modes or switch back to Opus (or worse, churn to Codex). Hot take if 
1. they did the auto-routing under the hood so users didn't cap out their limits so easily,
2. they were a bit more thoughtful on how they exception handle "science research" 
People would be happier active recurring users. 

Since the release, they've kinda addressed (2) and I'm sure they're working on (1) as we speak.

items.title:
## The Future Codebase

After the PR dies, after the Code Review dies,
i am seriously wondering if G...
items.content:
## The Future Codebase

After the PR dies, after the Code Review dies,
i am seriously wondering if Git needs to die next.

roughly 20-40% of code spend is just managing and updating merge conflicts. necessary evil? or legacy "horseless carriage"? cargo culting the past?

we don't do line by line merge conflicts when we collaborate with human colleagues - instead we chat, suggest edits, do side comments, and an owner ships it. 

btw we also don't do CI/CD even collaborating on documents with serious legal/financial implications.

maybe the future codebase looks more like a Notion or Linear database than .git objects. It will be less efficient, but more scalable. exactly the Salty Lesson.

items.title: @mikepat711 There is nothing to be gained by playing the PR game of showing off a robot that can do ...
items.content:
@mikepat711 There is nothing to be gained by playing the PR game of showing off a robot that can do a few things like dancing, fighting, or flipping boxes on a production line.

Truth is that the world models (or whatever new technology will soon be developed to make humanoids work) aren't ready yet for generalization. 

If I were Tesla I'd keep it quiet until it's much closer to be able to be generalized, and safe, for all businesses and homes. 

I've been talking with the folks building the models and even the most aggressive of them say it's 18 months away from generalization. Most say it's longer than that.

Tesla has one shot to really show off an autonomous robot that grabs everyone's attention. I won't be shocked if they stay quiet about it for 18 months. Or even longer.

They just closed down the X and S lines, and are building a new fab from scratch to build them. That, too, will take a year to two to really get some form and get it working. 

The IPO today has gone well, so watch how next year Tesla and SpaceX work together on Optimus. 

It will have its day.

I've been talking with the folks building the models and even the most aggressive of them say it's 18 months away from generalization. Most say it's longer than that.

items.title:
This game seems similar towards building any human organization/company:
- A human is just a set of ...
items.content:
This game seems similar towards building any human organization/company:
- A human is just a set of recursive loops 
- A team loops over humans for team-level OKRs
- A company loops over teams for company-level OKRs 

Imagine building an org that integrates humans and agents. In this case an "outer" agent loop would manage sub-agents but also other humans, dispatch tasks, review etc.etc.
items.author: @jerryjliu0
items.summary: Jerry Liu draws an analogy between agentic loops and human organizations, where individuals, teams, and companies are nested recursive loops, and imagines an outer agent loop managing both sub-agents and humans, dispatching and reviewing tasks.
items.reasoning: Thoughtful conceptual extension of the loop theme into hybrid human-agent orgs from a credible founder, modest engagement.
items.summary_html: Jerry Liu draws an analogy between agentic loops and human organizations, where individuals, teams, and companies are nested recursive loops, and imagines an outer agent loop managing both sub-agents and humans, dispatching and reviewing tasks.
items.content_html:
This game seems similar towards building any human organization/company:

  • A human is just a set of recursive loops
  • A team loops over humans for team-level OKRs
  • A company loops over teams for company-level OKRs

Imagine building an org that integrates humans and agents. In this case an "outer" agent loop would manage sub-agents but also other humans, dispatch tasks, review etc.etc.

items.title: People forget the dark days of X. Back when @elonmusk bought Twitter so many left, going over to new...
items.content:
People forget the dark days of X. Back when @elonmusk bought Twitter so many left, going over to new social networks. So many wrote me and said "you should quit X, it's over." 

Where are they now? 

Congrats to the employees who survived those dark days. I have a list of them all, many who are millionaires today: https://t.co/4VdFr5VmCl

I've been here 19 years, and see a whole range of things that are still to come.

Someday over next decade your autonomous car will tell you what's happening on X. Maybe an Optimus robot will be sitting next to you telling you about a new startup that just got revealed on X, or talk to you about your favorite football team. 

As I have been building a new agent with @blevlabs that reads all the AI community here (including robotics) at https://t.co/8L5xphk0qQ I see so many things that can be done with X that would be hard to do with any other social system. My lists have 8,800 AI companies, for instance. They aren't on most of the other social systems. And you can't see those social systems in real time. 

This gives @elonmusk the capital to continue investing in X. 

In a huge way Elon saved Twitter and now has made it the foundation of a whole bunch of new things to come in the future. 

It's why I don't post on the others and didn't leave during the dark times.

And, yes, we all know there are still many problems to be fixed here. Audio Spaces. Video viewing. Shopping. Paying your favorite creators. Grok still isn't hooked up to lists (and can't make new lists, which will be very important to having your robot be able to tell you about your favorite football team). 

Money is life for companies to do new things and congrats to everyone on a very huge day for X.

items.title: Anthropic's official guide for Claude Fable 5 says prompts written for prior models now degrade outp...
items.content:
Anthropic's official guide for Claude Fable 5 says prompts written for prior models now degrade output.

What to delete:

- Step-by-step instruction lists
- "Show your reasoning" (now triggers refusals)
- Remaining-token countdowns 
- Enumerated edge-case lists

What to add:

- Effort set per task 
- Verifier subagents 
- A boundary block 
- A memory file 
- The reason behind the request

A goal, context, boundaries, and a way to verify its own work. The rest is subtraction.

items.title:
"Major breakthrough in the fight against the common cold."

This lab takes pictures of viruses like ...
items.content:
"Major breakthrough in the fight against the common cold."

This lab takes pictures of viruses like the common cold.

Found a secret "lock" and the key (the RNA) inside of it. 

You think I know anything about all this? Nah, I'm just another idiot on X when it comes to biological sciences.

I just follow 50,000 smart people and then have Grok explain things that are way over my head to me. :-)

I saw "big breakthrough" and read more.

And we all get colds. 

So a major breakthrough deserves more than 40 reshares.

items.title: @mpvprb @mikepat711 Yeah, this is one thing I hear from the robotics industry consistently as I get ...
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scoble relays that the robotics industry consistently says world models for generalized humanoid robots remain years away, and that some hype and teleoperation like 1X Neo aims to gather data to finish those models.
items.reasoning: Substantive, balanced industry observation on humanoid robotics and world models, though low reach.
items.summary_html: Scoble relays that the robotics industry consistently says world models for generalized humanoid robots remain years away, and that some hype and teleoperation like 1X Neo aims to gather data to finish those models.
items.content_html:
@mpvprb @mikepat711 Yeah, this is one thing I hear from the robotics industry consistently as I get around and interview more and more people building in robotics: the world models needed to build a generalized humanoid robot are still off in the future.

And that is the real goal. A lot of the hype is to try to get money to finish off those world models, or to sell a robot like 1X's Neo that will be tele-operated in part in homes to get the data to finish off the world models.

I'll try to keep the hype down.

items.title: Are there toolkits (or skillsets) being created specifically for AIs to use for building games? They...
items.content:
Are there toolkits (or skillsets) being created specifically for AIs to use for building games? They default to 3js, reinvent how to make sprites from scratch each time, test technical issues but not gameplay loops, etc.

It would help to point AIs at some tools to focus them.

I've been using Apify for over a year. I've used over 50 different actors.

You use them to scrape t...
items.content:
I've been using Apify for over a year. I've used over 50 different actors.

You use them to scrape the web, get your data back, and that's it.

But now, with MCP support, you can also have those actors talk directly to servers and work with the data.

Example (the one in the video): Analyze a YouTube video and write the summary in a Notion page.

Another example: Scrape the school calendar and automatically add the events to your Google Calendar.

The possibilities are endless!

Best part of all this: I do it all using Claude Code + Apify skills.

items.title: In olmo-eval, every component is swappable: the model being evaluated, its tools, LLM-as-a-judge gra...
items.content:
In olmo-eval, every component is swappable: the model being evaluated, its tools, LLM-as-a-judge graders, & more. You can change one without touching the rest.

Benchmark results land in a uniform schema, so checkpoints stay comparable across a long project.
items.author: @allen_ai
items.summary: Allen AI describes olmo-eval's swappable components (model, tools, LLM-as-judge graders) and uniform result schema keeping checkpoints comparable across a project.
items.reasoning: Solid technical design detail of the eval framework but minimal engagement.
items.summary_html: Allen AI describes olmo-eval's swappable components (model, tools, LLM-as-judge graders) and uniform result schema keeping checkpoints comparable across a project.
items.content_html:
In olmo-eval, every component is swappable: the model being evaluated, its tools, LLM-as-a-judge graders, & more. You can change one without touching the rest.

Benchmark results land in a uniform schema, so checkpoints stay comparable across a long project.

items.title: Running every benchmark in a locked-down sandbox – as many eval platforms do – is compute-heavy. So ...
items.content:
Running every benchmark in a locked-down sandbox – as many eval platforms do – is compute-heavy. So olmo-eval instead treats benchmarks differently depending on their runtime needs. 

For example, a plain Q&A benchmark runs directly—faster & cheaper than sandboxing.
items.author: @allen_ai
items.summary: Allen AI explains that olmo-eval avoids running every benchmark in a heavy sandbox, instead matching benchmarks to their runtime needs so plain Q&A runs directly and cheaper.
items.reasoning: Practical efficiency design detail of the eval tool with low reach.

items.title: I have finished writing the first three chapters of my upcoming book, "The Hundred-Page Deep Reinfor...
items.content:
I have finished writing the first three chapters of my upcoming book, "The Hundred-Page Deep Reinforcement Learning Book." I'm preparing to put them online and am looking for volunteer early contributors to propose fixes, improvements, and ways to make the prose shorter without losing clarity.

As in my previous books, the names of all volunteer contributors will be printed in the book's Acknowledgments section.

The only requirement is to have a STEM education because the book assumes a certain minimum level of scientific and coding exposure.

Please DM me if you are interested in participating.

items.title:
@anulagarwal @marclou Yes I tried iOS but the amount of hassle involved is very tiring:
- pay yearly...
items.content:
@anulagarwal @marclou Yes I tried iOS but the amount of hassle involved is very tiring:
- pay yearly for an iOS developer account
- need to get app approval, even for every change you make
- give 30% of your money away (if you make over $1M/y which is the goal right)
- need to buy ads or UGC to get users
- need to get and manage iOS reviews/ratings to go up in app store
- have to update ur app and recompile every time new iOS update comes
- accept payments via iOS so now u manage another payment portal next to Stripe for web, double the hassle

items.title:
The bigger unlock is context awareness.

You can bring:
→ Multiple tabs
→ Documents
→ Images

Into o...
items.content:
The bigger unlock is context awareness.

You can bring:
→ Multiple tabs
→ Documents
→ Images

Into one query.

That means the system understands your entire research flow, not isolated searches.

This is what Chrome’s AI Mode is aiming for, and it changes how we learn and decide.

If this direction holds, “10 tabs” might feel as outdated as bookmarks.

Watch the full video.

Curious, how many tabs do you have open right now, and do you think that number goes to zero?

Don't miss out on the latest AI advancements! Sign up here to stay informed! https://t.co/MlFrT0maIM

items.title:
Here is the link to the notebook:

https://t.co/X2KWMj92Cu

This image search example is the basis f...
items.content:
Here is the link to the notebook:

https://t.co/X2KWMj92Cu

This image search example is the basis for solving a ton of different useful problems:

• Manufacturing quality control
• Medical diagnosis and screening
• Spotting counterfeit products
• Infrastructure inspection
• Security and surveillance

My notebook uses LangChain and Oracle's AI Database vector store to store image embeddings.

By the way, the database is packed with AI features. Here are some of the ones that impressed me the most:

1. You can run an LLM and an Embedding model directly from your database. This is huge because you don't need to send your data anywhere else.

2. You can now run hybrid vector searches: semantic and keyword searches directly in the database.

3. There's a new concept called JSON Relational Duality views, where you can see your data in both JSON and relational form. It's cool because you can stick to a relational model while taking advantage of JSON's flexibility.

4. They built-in code generation tools you can use to write SQL code on your database.

Check the other examples in the GitHub repository above. They have a ton of interesting examples on how to use Oracle 26ai and their new functionality.

Thanks to the Oracle team for partnering with me on this post.

By the way, the database is packed with AI features. Here are some of the ones that impressed me the most:

1. You can run an LLM and an Embedding model directly from your database. This is huge because you don't need to send your data anywhere else.

2. You can now run hybrid vector searches: semantic and keyword searches directly in the database.

3. There's a new concept called JSON Relational Duality views, where you can see your data in both JSON and relational form. It's cool because you can stick to a relational model while taking advantage of JSON's flexibility.

4. They built-in code generation tools you can use to write SQL code on your database.

Check the other examples in the GitHub repository above. They have a ton of interesting examples on how to use Oracle 26ai and their new functionality.

items.content:
I'm pretty excited to move off of Wise Business permanently after realizing I can't just transfer my money out when I need to

They keep my money hostage for 10-14 days at least for a big transfer, like they literally won't transfer my money

I remember when dinosaur banks did this? Like they'd purposely delay transfers or not transfer at all in the weekend to then make money on the interest of it

But now fintechs like Wise have started doing it too?

Obviously you don't want to use fintechs to store lots of money, but sometimes you receive money and you wanna transfer it elsewhere (like your broker) but Wise just delays that entire process by 2 weeks, which is quite mad!

Excited to move to @Stripe Business banking ASAP!

items.title:
What caught my attention is this new shift:

Your “assistant” stays inside the page you’re already o...
items.content:
What caught my attention is this new shift:

Your “assistant” stays inside the page you’re already on.

Instead of leaving, you can:
→ Ask questions instantly
→ Get explanations in context
→ Compare options without bouncing around

The result?
Your thinking stays linear, not fragmented.

items.title:
The real problem isn’t search. It’s context switching.

When I’m researching, my flow usually looks ...
items.content:
The real problem isn’t search. It’s context switching.

When I’m researching, my flow usually looks like:
→ Search
→ Open new tab
→ Scan
→ Go back
→ Repeat

That loop kills depth.

You’re not thinking, you’re juggling.

items.summary: Argues the real research problem is context switching, not search, describing a tab-juggling loop that kills depth.

items.title: Looking back at these clips from GTC Taipei and reminiscing on what an incredible week it was. There...
items.content:
Looking back at these clips from GTC Taipei and reminiscing on what an incredible week it was. There’s nothing quite like the buzz of the tech community coming together in person.

From the packed keynote hall and cutting-edge robotics to the fun local activations (and yes, those little lobster claw keychains 🦞), Taipei truly outdid itself. Thank you to everyone who smiled for the cameras, chatted with our avatars, and brought the future of AI to life.

Until next time, Taipei! 🇹🇼💚

items.content:
@car_dan20741 I see your point, but that would look like PowerPoint slides, which are usually hard to follow without the speaker.

That's why the hundred-page format is so good, especially today, when you can ask any question and get an instant answer: it gives you a structure that you can follow on your own, and then you can go deeper with an LLM if you feel so inclined.

There are 4,000 peo...
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Argues humanoid robots like Optimus currently do almost none of the 4,000 factory jobs, and 25 percent would signal real model progress.
items.reasoning: Concrete, skeptical framing on humanoid generalization, but modest reach.
items.summary_html: Argues humanoid robots like Optimus currently do almost none of the 4,000 factory jobs, and 25 percent would signal real model progress.
items.content_html:
@arvindakaraja @mikepat711 Generalization is even needed to work in factories.

There are 4,000 people working at the Tesla factory in Fremont where Optimus is being developed. How many jobs can Optimus currently do? Almost none.

When it can do, say, 25%, then we know the models are getting somewhere.

items.title:
RAG with LLM: Creating an AI-Powered File Reader! 

#BigData #Analytics #DataScience #AI #MachineLea...
items.content:
RAG with LLM: Creating an AI-Powered File Reader! 

#BigData #Analytics #DataScience #AI #MachineLearning #NLProc #IoT #IIoT #PyTorch #Python #RStats #TensorFlow #Java #JavaScript #ReactJS #GoLang #CloudComputing #Serverless #DataScientist #Linux #Programming #Coding #100DaysofCode 

This is the type of application I've already built earlier at the beginning of this year for a conference demonstration! 

GPT Paper-Reader, to show how you can leverage the summarization capabilities of large language models to retrieve research papers from databases, summarize their content, and engage in Q&A interactions with people.    The future is here.  In addition to the regular theoretical presentation, I'll be presenting a practical GPT. This application utilizes GPT, to effectively read and analyze complete academic papers:  Basically the app chunks the PDF paper into manageable sections for detailed reading and generates concise summaries for each segment. By maintaining context from previous sections within the token limit, it enhances comprehension. Before diving into the paper, you can outline specific questions in the prompt. This approach allows GPT to extract the most important information during its reading and summarizing process, leading to superior outcomes.   After summarizing all parts, you will receive comprehensive answers to your inquiries based on the consolidated summaries. By default, the initialized prompt will target essential points such as:  These inquiries are tailored for research articles within the computer science domain. Upon completion of the paper review, feel free to engage with the question() interface to ask further questions.

References 

Khan, A. A., Hasan, M. T., Kemell, K. K., Rasku, J., & Abrahamsson, P. (2025). Developing retrieval augmented generation (RAG) based LLM systems from PDFs: An experience report [Preprint]. arXiv. Retrieved April 3, 2025, from https://t.co/YCGfBCNY4v

Santos, G. (2025, March 3). LLM + RAG: Creating an AI-powered file reader assistant. Towards Data Science. Retrieved April 3, 2025, from https://t.co/YubIqDgyP8

items.title:
Just am having a new @tesla Powerwall 3 installed.

My dad was an electrical engineer and gave me a ...
items.content:
Just am having a new @tesla Powerwall 3 installed.

My dad was an electrical engineer and gave me a love of simple designs. Easier to manufacture and maintain too.

Here is to the engineers who keep things simple!

When I was in junior high back in the 1970s I worked with melted aluminum. It was a pain in the ass to get it to flow into a complex form.

Making the heat sink would have given me so many troubles. 

Makes me want to meet the people who built this and shake their hands.

Steve Jobs once asked me to look at the back of an iMac, so proud he was of the engineering effort it took.

Still is the only CEO who was proud of the back of a product.

The teams that built this should be proud too.

Every modern home should have one.

Or more.
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scoble celebrates installing a Tesla Powerwall 3 and praises simple engineering, recalling Steve Jobs and his own early aluminum casting work.
items.reasoning: Engaging personal essay on engineering but off-topic from AI despite moderate reach.

items.content:
GaN - GA - Gallium Nitride Processor. Ft.@gp_pulipaka by @Analyticsindiam 

This is how Gallium looks like.  The process of invention dates back to the year, 1874.  An ancient periodic element from the periodic table that we need to check. 

It has an atomic mass of 70 u with a melting point at 85.57 F. Gallium can form a number of compounds. Chief among them are: Gallium arsenide (GaA), Gallium nitride (GaN), and Gallium phosphide (GP).

It has an atomic mass of 70 u with a melting point at 85.57 F. Gallium can form a number of compounds. Chief among them are: Gallium arsenide (GaA), Gallium nitride (GaN), and Gallium phosphide (GP).

items.title: @hosein_obio @Tesla I was Apple's second child laborer and studied Apple from a close seat many time...
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scoble recounts his history with early Apple, Microsoft and Wozniak.
items.reasoning: Tech-history anecdote with mild interest but no AI relevance and tiny reach.
items.summary_html: Scoble recounts his history with early Apple, Microsoft and Wozniak.
items.content_html:
@hosein_obio @Tesla I was Apple's second child laborer and studied Apple from a close seat many times. I visited his product introductions a couple times and met him a couple times.

Keep in mind, I worked at Microsoft as a strategist and wrote eight books about the future. So, our paths crossed here and there.

I knew Waz a lot better, actually, because he made the motherboards we made in my mom's kitchen. That gave me a love of new things and the people who create them that continues to this day.

items.title: There are many possible takeaways but mine is that voters have no idea what they believe so politici...
items.content: There are many possible takeaways but mine is that voters have no idea what they believe so politicians trying to craft their policy planks around what's popular are committing a category error. You win elections by being someone who believes in something other than focus groups

items.title:
Setting this up takes a few seconds:

• Open your Apify settings > API & Integrations
• Connect your...
items.content:
Setting this up takes a few seconds:

• Open your Apify settings > API & Integrations
• Connect your tool (Notion, Google Calendar, ...)
• Authorize once

Your actors will now have direct read and write access to those services.

Here is a blog post with an explanation of how MCP connectors work in Apify: https://t.co/fjyHSTAx9O

A collection of MCP-enabled actors: https://t.co/FNjXK7QCvi

Thanks to the Apify team for partnering with me on this post.

items.summary: Yohei Nakajima posts a celebration emoji reply.

items.content_html:
Setting this up takes a few seconds:

• Open your Apify settings > API & Integrations

• Connect your tool (Notion, Google Calendar, ...)

• Authorize once

Your actors will now have direct read and write access to those services.

Here is a blog post with an explanation of how MCP connectors work in Apify: https://t.co/fjyHSTAx9O

A collection of MCP-enabled actors: https://t.co/FNjXK7QCvi

Thanks to the Apify team for partnering with me on this post.
